description: A 'kaworu' is damage or a health-relevant change in the body associated with an event or exposure. An injury may hurt, remain unnoticed, heal, or leave a lasting change. 'kipona' (pain) reports an experience, while injury reports the bodily change; cause and responsibility need their own evidence.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into open central 'a'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then continue into stressed true-mid back rounded 'o'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and finish with high-back rounded 'u'.
usage_notes: Count syntax selects a distinct injury. Without a count, 'kaworu' presents injury as a bodily condition. Location and timing can be added beside the event and the first-person account. A cut or burn can use its ordinary action word with 'kaworu'; a broken bone or strained joint can name the body part and its change.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The bodily change can be reported before anyone settles cause, blame, or responsibility for it.

semantic_domains.physical: damage or a health-relevant change in the body
